# Undernutrition & malnutrition

> Part IV opened with the diseases of *too much*; it closes with the other tail — *too little*. Malnutrition
> is not the same as looking thin: it is a **disease state**, a nutrient deficit that measurably changes the
> body and worsens outcomes, and in Singapore it hides in plain sight among frail, isolated and hospitalised
> older adults. The task here is to **recognise who to suspect, screen them, and refer** — food-first and
> social support started early — not to prescribe the feeding regimen, manage refeeding, or set a texture level.

## Undernutrition & malnutrition at a glance

This chapter is the narrative bookend to the *Obesity & Metabolic Syndrome* chapter: over- and under-nutrition
are the **two tails of malnutrition**, and the frail undernourished senior is as much a nutritional patient as
the person with metabolic syndrome. It also takes the handover from the *Healthy Ageing* chapter, whose seam is
sharp: **healthy ageing is ageing *in health*; this chapter is the disease state** — the pathological
under-tail that ageing, illness and isolation tip people into.

Three habits of mind run through it:

- **Malnutrition is a diagnosable disease, so screen for it — don't wait for it to look obvious.** It is common in the community, not just the ward, and is routinely missed [@cederholm2017espenTerminology; @wei2017slasFrailty].
- **A normal or high BMI does not exclude it.** Weight loss and acute illness harm nutrition independently of body size, and a person can be wasting muscle at a healthy weight [@bapenMUST; @cruzjentoft2019ewgsop2].
- **Screen → recognise → refer is the job.** Screening flags *risk* and triggers referral; the assessment, diagnosis and feeding plan are dietitian- and team-led [@jensen2019glim; @aclm2022comp].

## The science underneath: what malnutrition does to the body, and why inflammation changes it

Why is a wasted, weak patient a *medical* problem and not just a catering one? Because malnutrition is a causal
chain, not a body size. A **deficit** of energy, protein or micronutrients produces a measurable **change in
body composition** — loss of fat *and*, crucially, of muscle — which in turn causes a **functional and outcome
consequence**: weakness, falls, poor wound healing, more infections, longer hospital stays and higher mortality
[@cederholm2017espenTerminology]. The starvation side of this builds on the fasted-state biology of the
*Integrative Metabolism* chapter and the body-composition and nitrogen-balance ideas of the *Energy Balance &
Body Composition* and *Macronutrients* chapters — muscle is the body's mobilisable protein store, so a
sustained deficit spends it.

![Malnutrition is a causal chain, not a body size: a deficit of energy/protein (worsened by disease-driven inflammation and reduced intake) produces loss of fat and — crucially — muscle, which causes weakness, poor healing, infection, longer stays and higher mortality. The three etiologic categories sit on an inflammation gradient: pure starvation (no inflammation) → chronic disease-related (mild–moderate) → acute disease or injury (severe). Inflammation is why disease-related malnutrition resists feeding alone.](/figures/mechanisms/malnutrition-mechanism.svg){#fig-malnutrition width="92%" fig-align="center"}

The pivot that makes this a doctor's problem is **inflammation**. Malnutrition is classified by *cause* into
three types, distinguished by how much inflammation is present: **starvation-related** (a pure intake deficit,
no inflammation), **chronic disease-related** (mild-to-moderate inflammation — heart failure, cancer, kidney
disease), and **acute disease- or injury-related** (severe inflammation, the cachexia end) [@jensen2010etiologyMalnutrition;
@cederholm2017espenTerminology]. Inflammation drives catabolism and blunts appetite — the appetite regulation
of the *Appetite & Weight Regulation* chapter — which is why **disease-related
malnutrition resists feeding alone** — you cannot simply out-feed an inflammatory illness, and the underlying
disease has to be treated too. A concrete example: roughly **46%** of patients with heart failure are
malnourished, and malnutrition about doubles their mortality [@lv2021hfMalnutrition].

Sitting alongside this is **sarcopenia** — a *muscle* disorder (low strength confirmed by low muscle
quantity), related to but distinct from a nutritional deficit [@cruzjentoft2019ewgsop2]. The two overlap
(reduced muscle mass is a malnutrition feature, and undernutrition accelerates muscle loss), but a person can
be sarcopenic at a normal or high BMI — **sarcopenic obesity** — which is exactly why you must consider
*muscle*, not just weight. In Singapore, use **Asian (AWGS 2019) cut-offs**, since European values overestimate
the deficit in smaller-framed populations [@chen2020awgs2019; @limSarcopeniaCpg2022]. The muscle–bone overlap —
and the resistance-exercise and bone-loading side of protecting the frail patient — belongs to the *Bone & Joint
Health* chapter.

## Who to suspect: the recognition map

Because malnutrition is often silent, the skill is knowing *who to actively screen*. Raise your suspicion for:
**frail and pre-frail community seniors**; the **socially isolated, bereaved or living alone**; the
**chronically ill** (through the disease-related mechanism above); **hospital inpatients**; and anyone in the
vulnerable **post-discharge** window [@bapenAtRisk; @fried2001frailty]. Unintentional weight loss — "shrinking"
— is a core feature of frailty and one of the first things to ask about [@fried2001frailty]. Knowing the group
raises the pre-test probability and tells you to screen; it is the difference between catching malnutrition and
missing it.

## Screening: the part a generalist owns

Screening is the part of malnutrition care that a generalist *owns*. It is a fast, low-skill filter that anyone
can apply to flag risk and trigger referral — and it is deliberately **not** the assessment. The teaching
exemplar is **MUST** (the Malnutrition Universal Screening Tool), which adds three components: a **BMI** score,
an **unplanned weight-loss** score, and an **acute-disease effect** (acutely ill *and* little or no intake for
more than about five days). The scores sum to an overall risk — **low, medium or high** — each tied to an
action, from routine re-screening to referral and a care plan [@bapenMUST].

<div data-island="must-screen" data-src="/diagrams/must-screen.yml"></div>

The point the tool makes visible is the one worth keeping: **weight loss and acute illness score independently
of BMI**, so a patient with a perfectly normal — even high — BMI can screen as high-risk. That is why "she
looks fine, her BMI isn't low" is the classic miss. Match the tool to the setting (NRS-2002 for inpatients,
the MNA short-form for older adults, the WHO ICOPE vitality question in the community, and Singapore's locally
developed **3-MinNS**), but recognise that they all do the same job: flag risk, then refer [@kondrup2003nrs2002;
@rubenstein2001mnasf; @kaiser2009mnasf; @whoIcope; @lim2009minns].

::: {.callout-important}
## Screening is not diagnosis — and both are just the start of the referral
A positive screen means *document, monitor and refer* — it is not a diagnosis and not a care plan. Formal
diagnosis uses the **GLIM** (Global Leadership Initiative on Malnutrition) logic: after a positive screen,
malnutrition is diagnosed on **at least one
phenotypic criterion** (weight loss, low BMI, reduced muscle mass) **plus at least one etiologic criterion**
(reduced intake or absorption, or inflammation/disease burden) — "a phenotype of loss plus a cause"
[@cederholm2019glim; @jensen2019glim]. But GLIM sits *downstream* of screening and is done by the dietitian or
team. The generalist's job is the screen and the referral, not the grading.
:::

## The team, with the dietitian at the hub

Nutrition support is a team activity. The **dietitian** does the assessment and the plan; the **doctor** treats
the underlying cause and champions nutrition by referring; the **nurse** screens and monitors intake; the
**speech therapist** assesses swallowing and texture; **physiotherapy and occupational therapy** restore the
function that makes eating stick; the **medical social worker** tackles the social drivers; and the
**pharmacist** watches drug–nutrient and electrolyte safety [@sgPostDischarge2023; @niceCG32NST]. The doctor's
distinctive move is not to write the feeding plan but to **spin the wheel by referring** — which is the
recognise-and-refer posture this book teaches, and the reason nutrition is best handled as inter-professional
teamwork rather than solo prescribing [@aclm2022comp; @crowley2019].

## Oral nutrition support: a ladder, not a supplement drink

When intake is poor, the response is a **ladder**, and the first rung is not a supplement. Step one is
**food-first**: fix the *cause* of poor intake — isolation, poor dentition, low mood, a difficult swallow,
finances, or appetite-blunting drugs — and optimise ordinary food. Step two is **food fortification**:
enriching familiar dishes so each mouthful carries more energy and protein *without more volume* (adding egg,
minced fish, tofu, milk powder, oil or full-fat dairy), which can lift intake substantially [@bapenFoodFirst;
@hpbSeniors]. Only then, step three, come **oral nutritional supplements (ONS)** — the supplement drinks — as
an evidence-based **add-on** when food-based measures fall short, not the opening move [@espenGeriatrics2019;
@espenGeriatricsPractical2022]. Enteral and parenteral feeding are specialist rungs above that, and are
signposted, not taught.

The ONS evidence is worth stating plainly, because supplement drinks are often over-reached for. In at-risk
older people they produce a **small, consistent weight gain and fewer complications, but no significant overall
mortality benefit** [@milne2009ons]. Singapore's own SHIELD trial found a specialised supplement improved a
composite of nutritional outcomes — but that result was **driven by the weight component and was an add-on *to*
dietary counselling**, not a replacement for it [@shieldChew2021]. So ONS is a genuine tool, used after
food-first and by a dietitian, not a drink that fixes malnutrition on its own.

![Oral nutrition support is a ladder. Start with food-first (fix the cause of poor intake and optimise ordinary food), then food fortification (more energy and protein per mouthful, no extra volume), then oral nutritional supplements as an add-on; enteral and parenteral feeding are specialist rungs above. The generalist screens and refers up to the lower rungs — the plan itself is dietitian-led.](/figures/mechanisms/oral-support-ladder.svg){#fig-ons-ladder width="72%" fig-align="center"}

::: {.callout-tip}
## 🍜 Eating out (and eating in)
The local food-first lever is fortifying **familiar hawker and home dishes**, since many older Singaporeans eat
soft, refined starch with too little protein. Add an egg, minced fish, tofu or extra chicken to **rice
porridge**; enrich **mee sua** or soup noodles with egg and protein toppings; choose higher-protein **yong tau
foo** items (fish paste, tofu, egg) over plain vegetables; reach for full-fat milk and dairy. HPB's guidance
for seniors is built on exactly this — nutrient density over quantity, more protein, softer textures, more
fluids [@hpbSeniors].
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## Do no harm: don't feed the starved patient up too fast
The instinct to "build them up quickly" is dangerous. In someone who has eaten little for a long time,
restarting feeding — especially carbohydrate — triggers an insulin surge that drives phosphate, potassium and
magnesium into cells (on top of thiamine depletion), causing **refeeding syndrome**: a fall in blood
phosphate that can bring cardiac and neurological harm [@deSilva2020Refeeding; @daSilva2020ASPEN]. The
job here is to **recognise the at-risk patient** — very low BMI, large recent weight loss, prolonged low
intake, low baseline electrolytes, or a high-risk history (alcohol, chemotherapy) — and **refer for cautious,
monitored refeeding** [@niceCg32]. Managing it (the electrolyte and thiamine replacement, the calorie
titration) is specialist, later learning.
:::

## Dysphagia and texture-modified diets: red flags, refer, and mind the trade-off

A difficult swallow is a common, dangerous driver of undernutrition, and it has recognisable **red flags**:
coughing or choking on food or drink, a "wet" gurgly voice after swallowing, meals that drag on, recurrent
chest infections, and weight loss or dehydration. The trap is thinking "no cough means it's safe" — up to a
third of aspiration is **silent**, with no cough at all — so refer on the *pattern*, not just the cough
[@wirth2016dysphagiaOlder]. The **speech therapist** assesses the swallow and sets the safe texture level;
the standardised common language for those levels is **IDDSI**, a colour-coded 0–7 framework so that "Level 4"
means the same thing in every ward and home [@cichero2017iddsi; @cichero2020iddsi2].

The trade-off to remember is that texture modification buys swallow *safety* but tends to *lower* intake —
pureed and soft diets deliver less energy and calcium — so the answer is **fortified** texture-modified food
with monitoring, not a pureed diet treated as problem-solved [@wu2020tmd; @espenGeriatricsPractical2022].
Singapore has adopted IDDSI as its national standard through the MOH-led **EatSafe SG** programme, spanning
hospitals, community hospitals, nursing homes and social service agencies [@mohEatSafeSg; @iddsiSingapore].

## The Singapore picture

**The under-tail begins in the community, not the ward.** In the population-based Singapore Longitudinal Ageing
Studies, about **1 in 4 community-dwelling older adults (27.6%) were at malnutrition risk** and nearly half
were pre-frail [@wei2017slasFrailty]. Malnutrition and frailty are related but distinct, and they travel
together — and the harm concentrates where they *co-occur*: poor nutrition combined with frailty raised
mortality several-fold in the local cohort, whereas poor nutrition alone was a weaker signal
[@wei2018slasOutcomes]. The teaching frame is to screen the *frail* senior for malnutrition specifically.

**Social eating is a Singapore lever.** In a local cohort, **24.1% of older adults ate alone most of the time
although only 6.7% lived alone** — living *with* family is not the same as sharing a meal — and eating alone
tracks poorer diet quality and higher malnutrition risk [@ye2023daha; @wyman2025eatingAlone]. Loneliness (more
than objective isolation) predicts frailty locally [@geSocialFrailtySg2022], and one-person households among
seniors have more than doubled in a decade [@mohSeniorsAlone2026]. So *"who do you eat with?"* is a nutrition
question, and the answer often points to a social referral — communal dining and befriending through **Age Well
SG** and the Active Ageing Centres — as much as a dietetic one [@mohAgeWellSg2023; @aacSingapore2025].

**The hospital and post-discharge window is high-risk.** Among hospitalised older Singaporeans,
**about 35% are malnourished at admission and about half by discharge**, and it persists for weeks afterwards
[@sgPostDischarge2023]. The recommended model is to screen every patient early and let any clinician refer a
dietitian directly — though local dietetic capacity is genuinely scarce, which is another reason food-first and
social support are started early. Singapore's own screening tool, **3-MinNS** (3-Minute Nutrition Screening),
was developed and validated at
the National University Hospital and can be administered by nurses — a concrete local example of the MUST-style
concept [@lim2009minns; @lim2013nurseScreening]. Nationally, about **1 in 4 older adults fall below minimum
calorie needs and about half below protein needs**, and standardised screening in polyclinics and Active Ageing
Centres is under active discussion — a direction of travel, not yet a settled mandate [@mohMalnutritionTracking2025;
@mohPolyclinicScreening2026].

::: {.callout-important}
## You screen and refer — you don't prescribe the feeding plan
There is no feeding regimen to memorise here, and that is the point. The generalist **screens** (MUST or a
local tool), **recognises** the at-risk patient and the red flags (weight loss, poor intake, dysphagia, a
social driver, refeeding risk), starts **food-first and social support**, and **refers** to the dietitian and
the team [@sgPostDischarge2023; @aclm2022comp]. The dietetic assessment and GLIM diagnosis, the ONS and enteral
prescriptions, refeeding management, and IDDSI level-setting are all team- and specialist-led — the altitude
this chapter is pitched at.
:::

## So — what does "treating malnutrition" look like for a generalist?

It looks like *catching it*. You suspect the frail, isolated, ill or recently discharged patient; you **screen**
rather than wait for a starved appearance; you remember that a normal BMI does not clear them; you start
**food-first** and fix the social and practical causes early; you **refer** to the dietitian and the team; and
you do no harm — you don't feed a starved patient up too fast, and you refer a risky swallow to the speech
therapist. With that, both tails of malnutrition have been told: the *too much* that opened Part IV, and the
*too little* that closes it — two faces of the same idea, that what and how a person eats shapes whether they
stay well.

## Check your understanding

<div data-island="quiz" data-src="/quizzes/undernutrition-malnutrition.quiz.yml"></div>

## Practise: Mdm Tan, losing weight and living alone

Mdm Tan is 82, widowed last year, and lives alone. Her daughter has noticed looser clothes and unfinished meals;
she has knee osteoarthritis, eats alone most days, and has lost about 7% of her weight over four months — her
BMI is now 18.9 and she is eating very little. Work through the visit: decide what raises your suspicion, screen
her, choose the right first-line response, and notice where "building her up quickly" would be a genuine
do-no-harm mistake. Your choices branch the case.

<div data-island="case" data-src="/cases/mdm-tan-malnutrition.case.yml"></div>
